IC.rationale: Account holds together structurally but contains notable inconsistencies in detail and sequence. The first encounter narrative is stable, but the second encounter shows some timeline confusion and shifting descriptions of the creature's morphology. The witness describes the creature as 'baboon-like' with 'long arms,' then later clarifies it had 'horse muscle with dog legs'—this suggests genuine memory reconstruction rather than scripted consistency, but creates ambiguity. The Yorkshire Terrier's fate is stated as definite loss ('never saw that dog again'), yet earlier suggests uncertainty about whether it 'flew off to the other end.' Minor gap: the witness says he didn't think about the first encounter when hearing splashing, yet earlier demonstrates clear memory linking.
evidence_quotes: It was like a baboon, like, whether long or ranting...the monkey family, the ones that's got the long arms and got the back that goes down
Similar to an horse and a dog. I'd say it had like the muscle of an horse, but the thin leg of a dog
I never saw that dog again, which is very hurtful
vs. earlier uncertainty about creature's destination

SS.rationale: Rich multi-sensory detail across 4+ channels: auditory (growl, panting, splashing, yelping), tactile (cold, wet nose, ankle pain, grabbing reeds), olfactory (swampy stench), visual (two eyes reflection, shadow/shape, blood running down leg). Strong peripheral context for first encounter (club setting, uncle's autism, takeaway plans, specific roads). Second encounter includes environmental detail (tall grass/fins, muddy bank, steep hill, reeds/weeds). However, some sensory descriptions remain generic or partially obscured ('swampy horrible stench' lacks specificity; creature's form described through comparison rather than direct sensory language). Peripheral detail drops somewhat in second encounter's climax.
evidence_quotes: I could hear panting. I could hear footsteps...like crunching, like stomping through like holly bushes, which are quite prickly
I heard splosh, splosh, splosh...it was so cold...like a wet nose like a wet nose going
I saw the blood running down back of my leg...swampy, horrible stench
Bush Butterfield Working Men Club...down the road...Beacon Road...St Enoch's Road...26 St Enoch's Road

SA.rationale: Multiple unprompted details and natural self-corrections present. Witness volunteers extensive bird-feeding background (niger seed, fat balls, monkey nuts, parakeet rescue with 'Bo') entirely unprompted—typical of genuine memory overflow. Self-corrects on creature description ('I said baboon...I mean the arms...rantans or you get'). Admits uncertainty throughout ('I don't know what you call them...big long grass fins'; 'I don't know...whether long or ranting'; 'I'm not sure which one'). Non-linear account structure: jumps to post-encounter emotional aftermath, loops back to clarify context. Spontaneously volunteers the uncle as corroborator mid-narrative. Some reactive elements (answers host questions), but primary narrative drives unprompted.
evidence_quotes: When I say crunching, I don't mean like crunching of teeth...I mean like crunching, like stomping through like holly bushes
I don't know what you call them but like big long grass fins
It was like a baboon, like, whether long or ranting, as you call it, whatever
I knew, to calm him down. We just kept walking. And he was trying to make out that he didn't like to believe things. he didn't like it and he knew that I was worried

EA.rationale: Emotional content is anchored to specific narrative moments and physiologically authentic. Clear emotional break when describing the creature's approach: 'my heart was racing. It was pulping. It was proper, proper, proper racing. So much that I couldn't really breathe.' Post-encounter emotional impact explicitly described: 'it really affected me in a big way...I won't talk to people for a good 10 days...I was like in shock...really, really down.' Disclosure delay substantial (12 years for first encounter; second encounter kept secret until sister's exposure to podcast). Long-term life changes documented: went on antidepressants, avoids woods, jumps at crow sounds, avoiding dark nights. Voice quality markers present in transcript ('oh,' 'oh my days this is my time I'm gone'). However, some emotional descriptions lack immediate narrative anchoring (broader anxiety statements late in interview), and emotional content becomes somewhat philosophical/abstract in final sections rather than remaining concretely tied to moment-specific triggers.
evidence_quotes: It was the most unexplainable noise. It was awful. So shocking. and I sort of shivered a bit
my heart was racing. It was pulping. It was proper, proper, proper racing. So much that I couldn't really breathe
it really affected me in a big way that I won't talk to people for a good 10 days. I was like in shock
i went on antidepressants because i was closing down not telling people
it'd make me jump...it does affected me yes

CP.rationale: Context is well-established and internally consistent. First encounter: legitimate reason to be there (celebrating new mortgage at working men's club with uncle), coherent geography (Bush Butterfield Club → Beacon Road → Woodside direction → Whipsy Park → St Enoch's Road flat). Timeline plausible (12 years ago, age 26-27). Second encounter: logical reason (visiting friend, taking Yorkshire Terrier to care for it), geography detailed (Norwood Green, Station Road, Judy Woods, Woodside), seasonal plausibility (New Year's Day, dark by 3-4pm matches UK winter). Activity/time/season coherent. Woods described with reasonable detail (8 miles, runs from Tesco's Halifax Road to Halifax area). One minor ambiguity: the witness says 'this was New Year's Day' for second encounter but doesn't clarify which year, though context suggests some years after first encounter.

Uncle's post-encounter behavior change documented (never stayed over, got taxi home despite usually walking, behavior change verified by witness observation). Indicates willingness to provide uncle's testimony. Sister is secondary corroborator for context (listening to podcast, living with witness, facilitating exposure to Dogman Encounters show). However, no physical evidence presented, no official reports filed, no third-party independent witness accounts included. Prior area reports mentioned ('history in these woods...pigs and all sorts of things happening') but not detailed or substantiated. Corroboration limited to one named potential witness (uncle) who is not directly interviewed.

notable_patterns: Witness demonstrates genuine uncertainty markers ('I don't know,' 'I'm not sure') rather than false confidence
Spontaneous elaboration on unrelated topic (bird feeding, parakeet 'Bo') suggests unconstrained memory access
Post-encounter behavioral changes align with documented trauma responses (social withdrawal, avoidance, hypervigilance)
Disclosure delay of 12 years for first encounter, undisclosed period for second, consistent with shame/disbelief barrier

Uncle's behavioral corroboration (taxi home, later anxiety around dogs/loud noises) provides secondary validation layer

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the Strong band, primarily driven by authentic emotional anchoring, genuine sensory specificity across multiple channels, spontaneous unprompted detail, and documented post-encounter life disruption. Internal consistency is good with natural memory reconstruction artifacts rather than fabrication markers. Context is well-grounded. Primary weaknesses are in corroboration (one named but uninterviewed witness) and some loss of sensory specificity in creature description (reliance on animal comparisons). The witness's explicit awareness of social credibility barriers and his 12-year disclosure delay are psychologically consistent with genuine traumatic memory suppression. The secondary corroboration (uncle's behavioral change, sister's supporting testimony about podcast exposure) and the witness's voluntary offer to produce uncle as witness strengthen credibility. Notable: witness went on antidepressants post-encounter, documented in interview, which is a high-cost claim unlikely to be fabricated. The spontaneous bird-feeding digression and natural self-corrections are authentic memory markers. Overall profile is consistent with genuine experiential trauma memory rather than confabulation or deliberate fabrication.

IC.rationale: The account of the recent juvenile dogman encounter holds together internally with no major contradictions. However, there are notable inconsistencies when cross-referencing the stated encounter details: Jason says the creature was 'about four feet tall' and 'standing on its hind legs,' yet earlier describes seeing 'its nose and...two really large black pupiled eyes' through a 'dense bush' that is 'tall like a tree.' The spatial geometry (how a 4-foot creature's face would be visible at eye level through a tall dense bush) is unclear. Additionally, Jason mentions the encounter 'only lasted for about a minute' but the narrative describes multiple sequential events (sniffing, staring, tracking, turning, approaching bush, hearing movement, 30-second pause, limb snap, visual contact, slow backing away) that would require considerably longer. Minor timeline inconsistencies in a freshly-recalled event are normal, but the compressed duration contradicts the detail-rich sequence described.
evidence_quotes: It looked to be about four feet tall...I think it was standing on its hind legs
I saw its nose and I saw...two really large black pupiled eyes
It only lasted for about a minute. But Scooter was scared
I heard two limbs snap and it was probably about 10 feet in front of me and I saw through the bush

SS.rationale: The direct encounter narrative (recent juvenile) contains some sensory specificity across 2-3 channels (visual and auditory), with reasonable peripheral detail about the setting (bush description, coastline location, Golden Gate Park area). Visual detail is moderately specific: 'nose,' 'two really large black pupiled eyes,' 'small' facial features indicating juvenility. However, sensory texture is limited. Notably absent: no smell reported despite close proximity; no tactile sensation; no kinesthetic response beyond 'slowly backed away'; no taste. The dog's reaction (shaking, tucked tail) provides corroborating sensory anchoring. The collected witness reports read as largely visual-only summaries (7-foot creature, red glowing eyes, etc.) with minimal sensory texture. The Iceland account is particularly thin: 'wolf standing there upright...looked at me. Then I ducked down.' No multi-channel sensory encoding in the secondary reports.
evidence_quotes: I saw its nose and I saw...two really large black pupiled eyes. And it looked to be about four feet tall
its face wasn't very large. Its nose was small. The eyes were a little bigger than that of a coyote's
I heard something moving through this tree bush...I heard two limbs snap
Scooter started shaking like a little bit...had his tail tucked
I saw a wolf standing there upright as if it was completely normal. It looked at me. Then I ducked down

SA.rationale: The interview is largely reactive to host prompting ('Please tell us about it. Tell us everything about what happened'). Jason provides unprompted spontaneous details during the direct encounter narrative ('he started sniffing in that direction...he had a look on his face like there's something there'), and self-corrects once ('I don't recall what they're called, but they are pretty prominent here in San Francisco'). However, the overall structure is highly linear and formatted as a polished recounting. The collected witness reports are read verbatim from written summaries, which is entirely scripted. Jason admits some uncertainty ('I think it was a juvenile...I kind of just determined') but does not manifest the hesitancy, non-linear structure, or multiple self-corrections typical of genuine traumatic recall. The account reads as a prepared presentation rather than spontaneous testimony.
evidence_quotes: I don't recall what they're called, but they are pretty prominent here in San Francisco
I think it was juvenile because its face wasn't very large
I kind of ignored Scooter's alerts that he was giving
so i know there is there is a sasquatch in that area well i think it's a sasquatch so maybe something happened to its parents i don't know

EA.rationale: Emotional content is present but largely disconnected from narrative triggers and lacks physiological authenticity markers. Jason states he is 'out of sorts right now, but I am getting better' at the start, but this is generic and not tied to the recent encounter. During the direct encounter narrative, there is no recorded emotional break, voice quality shift, or physiological marker (no tremor, no breathlessness, no pause for composed recovery). The account is delivered in calm, measured tone throughout. However, regarding the deceased hiker case, Jason states 'my thoughts and my prayers really went out to his family' and 'that was unfortunately taken from him' with brief narrative anchoring ('it's just a really sad case'). This represents moderate emotional authenticity to a secondary case, not the primary encounter. Most critically: no evidence of disclosure delay (Jason is actively sharing on podcast and has created a research organization), no indication of post-encounter life disruption beyond the stated goal of 'helping folks.' Scooter's fear reaction is reported but Jason's own emotional state during the encounter is not described.
evidence_quotes: I'm out of sorts right now, but I am getting better
my thoughts and my prayers really went out to his family and his friends that lost him...it's just a really sad case
Scooter was scared. He was shaking. he had his tail tucked he's never done that before
it was really strange because um it was by itself

CP.rationale: The context is largely plausible. Jason is a resident of San Francisco who regularly conducts field investigations in Golden Gate Park (a real, large park spanning 2x Central Park in size), which is an appropriate venue for dogman research. The recent encounter setting is coherent: 'area that I regularly go to to look for footprints,' near coastline, with wooded areas and dense vegetation. The time frame ('last week') and activity (footprint investigation) are sensible. Scooter's presence as a research companion is established. However, one contextual oddity: Jason states he 'had an encounter with one last week' yet this is ostensibly the same encounter described immediately after—the narrative presents it as a single recent event, not multiple encounters within a week. Additionally, the claim that Golden Gate Park is 2x the size of Central Park is verifiable (Golden Gate Park is ~1,017 acres vs. Central Park's ~843 acres—roughly 1.2x, not 2x), suggesting possible exaggeration or false recollection of basic facts about his primary investigation location. This is a minor but notable context plausibility erosion.
evidence_quotes: I was out doing an investigation in an area that I regularly go to to look for footprints
the area that I was at was near the coastline...pretty prominent here in San Francisco
Golden Gate Park is two times the size of Central Park in New York
I had an encounter with one last week. It was pretty trippy

CO.rationale: The direct encounter is entirely uncorroborated by any independent source. Jason was alone with only his dog Scooter present. No photographs were taken ('right before I took out my phone and was going to take pictures...I had this feeling'). No third-party witness. No physical evidence presented. Scooter's fear reaction could indicate genuine distress, but animal behavioral response cannot independently verify an encounter with a specific creature. The collected witness reports are secondhand accounts submitted to Jason's Facebook page—they are anecdotal, unverified, and do not corroborate Jason's personal experiences. The one semi-corroborating element is that Jason reports multiple encounters with what he believes is the same juvenile over time, and Scooter's consistent fearful response might suggest consistent stimuli, but this is internal consistency, not external corroboration. No law enforcement involvement, no physical evidence (tracks, biological material), no independent investigator validation, no prior area reports from official sources cited.
evidence_quotes: right before I took out my phone and was going to take pictures, I had this feeling that there was something behind me
So that's how i know that that really happened and that creature was really there so on episode 437 you told us what kind of dog scooter is
People send me encounter stories as well. So I will read into those and see what I can learn
it's one that just happened February of this year...local law enforcement didn't suspect foul play said that his death was accidental

notable_patterns: Account is presented in interview format with significant host prompting, reducing spontaneity measure
Secondary witness reports are read verbatim from written submissions, highly scripted
Direct personal encounter lacks independent corroboration or physical evidence
Emotional response is subdued and not anchored to specific narrative moments
Mild factual error regarding Golden Gate Park size (stated as 2x Central Park when actually ~1.2x)
Timeline inconsistency: encounter described as 'about a minute' but narrative contains 6+ sequential events
Dog's behavioral response cited as proof, but animal fear alone cannot verify specific creature
Heavy reliance on organizational credibility-building (Dogman Study page, 27 collected reports, ~500 followers) which can indicate narrative motivation
No sensory detail beyond visual; notably absent: smell despite close proximity to creature
Disclosure made through public platform (podcast), not to trusted individual after delay

accounts.scoring_notes: This transcript represents an interview with a dogman researcher recounting both a personal encounter and curated secondhand reports. The scoring reflects the direct encounter testimony (recent juvenile dogman in Golden Gate Park area). The account shows moderate structural coherence with traumatic memory but exhibits patterns more consistent with a practiced narrative than immediate experiential recall: calm delivery, no emotional breaks, linear structure, heavy host-prompt dependency, unverified claims (park size), minor timeline compression, and absence of standard trauma markers (disclosure delay, life disruption, multi-sensory encoding). The dog's fear provides some internal corroboration but cannot independently verify the encounter. The overall profile is consistent with a sincere belief in the encounter and genuine interest in dogman research, but the testimony structure diverges from forensic expectations for unprocessed traumatic memory. Weighted Credibility Score reflects moderate-to-weak alignment with genuine traumatic recall markers.

IC.rationale: Account maintains stable core narrative across four encounters, but contains notable timing inconsistencies and descriptive drift. The first childhood encounter is recalled with claimed perfect clarity ('remember every detail as if it was yesterday') yet also frames it as one of very few childhood memories despite 'very abusive' background—this is internally contradictory. The second encounter's timeline is vague ('I'm guessing...2019, something like that') and the dog's behavior is described retrospectively with confidence despite memory uncertainty acknowledged elsewhere. The third/fourth encounters (prints and eyeshine) are internally consistent with each other but the witness conflates elapsed time ('about a year later') without precision. Minor inconsistency: claims 'two tracks...two right next to each other' with 'two' prints visible, but later rationalizes absence of four prints as evidence—this reasoning muddies rather than clarifies observation.
evidence_quotes: I remember every detail as if it was yesterday. Now that I'm 44, I remember every detail as if it was yesterday.
I'm guessing it was like 2019, something like that.
we saw two next to each other we did not see four
I don't have a lot of memories as a child because I grew up in a very, very abusive and horrible childhood. But the crazy part is that I can remember that day, that morning, as if it was yesterday

SS.rationale: Testimony demonstrates multi-channel sensory detail with strong visual specificity across encounters, particularly the second encounter's description of the black void-shaped figure and the tracking experience. Visual detail is rich: 'brown', 'yellow eyes', 'black nose', 'jagged teeth' (first encounter); 'black void shape', 'muscular body', 'muscular legs' (second); 'blotches of snow', 'dog print', 'toenails' (third). Auditory elements present: rustling in woods, voices/dialogue. However, peripheral sensory channels are limited—no reported smells, minimal tactile detail beyond the childhood scratch claim, no kinesthetic descriptions of movement sensation. Environmental context is provided but somewhat generic (street lamps, moonlight, river presence). The account privileges visual observation over truly integrated sensory immersion.
evidence_quotes: It was brown. It was complete full of hair. The eyes were yellow, the nose was black...kind of a snarl, very white, jagged teeth.
I saw this black shape...the blackness literally looked like I was looking into a black void. I saw no details I saw no fur
the long thigh leg going into the bottom part of the leg
we look down at the track and what we see captivated us...the biggest thing we've ever seen...gigantic...the general pad of the dog print and we see you've got the toenails

SA.rationale: Witness demonstrates moderate spontaneity with unprompted expansions on context and character (the dogs' personalities, friendship with Josh, personal background). However, the account is largely reactive to the implied structure of a formal interview/podcast. Self-corrections are minimal: 'I'm guessing' appears multiple times but functions as hedging rather than genuine self-correction. Uncertainty admissions are present but formulaic ('I don't know', 'I would say'). The narrative follows a heavily linear, chronologically organized structure (childhood → adult encounters 1-4 → reflective closing). The closing section on wanting to help people with trauma appears scripted and detaches from the experiential narrative. Limited evidence of the non-linear, fragmentary quality typical of genuine traumatic memory retrieval.
evidence_quotes: And this is where it gets really strange.
And that's where it gets crazy
I'm guessing it was about I'm six foot just under six foot one
I would say it was about one or two o'clock in the morning
I'm not a psychologist. I'm not a social worker, but I'm a good listener. I like to believe that I'm intelligent...

EA.rationale: Emotional content is present with some narrative anchoring. Fear is described at appropriate moments (childhood encounter: 'absolutely afraid I was terrified'; fourth encounter: 'overwhelming sense of fear'). The fourth encounter shows physiologically congruent response ('snap your fingers', 'in that instant', 'praying for strength') suggesting acute stress response. However, emotional intensity varies inconsistently—the childhood encounter trauma is intellectualized in retrospect rather than re-experienced; adult encounters are described with calm reportorial tone despite described terror. The testimony shows post-encounter impact (ongoing discussion with Josh, changed belief framework) but emotional display lacks the voice quality breaks, hesitations, or bodily markers typically present in genuine trauma disclosure. The closing pivot to therapeutic helping mission, while potentially authentic, shifts affect away from encounter material.
evidence_quotes: it was absolutely a nightmare. It was my very first experience, but it was an absolute nightmare.
I felt this overwhelming sense of fear. I felt this overwhelming sense of just leave, get out of here, go, go, go.
I started praying for strength.

CP.rationale: Context is largely plausible and internally consistent. St. Paul/Minneapolis Twin Cities geography is accurate. Urban apartment setting with nearby highway (I-94) is contextually sound. Mississippi River park location with paved trails, wooded areas, and police canine training facility across river aligns with actual Minneapolis parks geography. Night outings for camping/fire by outdoorsmen is coherent. April/May timeframe for melting snow is seasonally appropriate for Minnesota. Only minor inconsistency: witness describes living in apartment for 'almost 10 years' with dogs that 'passed away as about two and a half years ago' and encounter dated to 2019—arithmetic suggests encounter was ~5 years prior, but witness frames it as recent memory, creating slight temporal blur. Childhood apartment in St. Paul projects is plausible. No major contextual contradictions.

CO.rationale: Partial corroboration present. The third and fourth encounters were witnessed jointly with named individual 'Josh' (best friend, fourth floor neighbor), providing a second witness to at least those two events. However, Josh's skepticism is noted ('he's more of a skeptic', 'not gonna 100 believe until I see it myself'), and no independent verification from Josh is included in testimony. The childhood encounter and second encounter (with dogs) are solo accounts with no corroboration mentioned. The footprint evidence (third encounter) was observed by both men but no physical evidence was collected or documented. No mention of reporting encounters to authorities, wildlife experts, or documentation of scratch marks from childhood incident (though witness claims 'a few weeks' of visible scratches, this is uncorroborated). No prior area reports mentioned. One corroborating witness present for two of four encounters, but without independent verification.

he's more of a skeptic he's more i'm not i'm not gonna 100 believe until i see it myself

I remember every detail as if it was yesterday. Now that I'm 44, I remember every detail as if it was yesterday...But being 44 and looking back

notable_patterns: Claimed perfect recall of 40-year-old childhood memory contradicts normal memory development and the witness's own admission of limited childhood memories
Emotional tone flattens significantly during encounter descriptions despite reported terror
Repeated use of hedging language ('I would say', 'I'm guessing', 'I would imagine') reduces concrete claim weight
Four-encounter narrative structure feels pedagogically organized rather than organically disclosed
Closing narrative shift to therapeutic mission and personal trauma history may indicate secondary motivation for disclosure beyond encounter reporting
Co-witness (Josh) explicitly skeptical and not independently verified
Visual descriptions are vivid but sensory integration is primarily ocular; minimal tactile, olfactory, or proprioceptive detail
Timeline vagueness increases from childhood (clear year) to adulthood (estimated 2019, 'about a year later')
Dogs' behavioral responses described retrospectively as pattern ('70 to 85% of the time') rather than from contemporaneous notes
minimisation_phrases: It was very brief
it's quick. It doesn't last long
And that's where it ends. it disappears
accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the Moderate band, reflecting genuine structural elements (multi-witness corroboration on two events, coherent geography, emotional anchoring at narrative junctures) alongside significant weaknesses (perfect recall of childhood trauma 40 years later contradicting established memory science, emotional flatness during narration of terror, heavy hedging language, secondary narrative motivation in closing section). The testimony shows signs consistent with genuine memory—spontaneous detail expansion, contextual consistency, peer corroboration—but also traits suggestive of constructed or retrospectively rationalized narrative: linear organization, lack of sensory integration beyond visual, minimal physiological stress markers during vocal delivery, and the intrusion of therapeutic/redemptive framing unrelated to encounters themselves. The most credible elements are the third and fourth encounters (shared witness, specific environmental detail, congruent stress response in fourth), while the childhood encounter and isolated second encounter lack corroboration and contain memory-science red flags. Not dismissible as fabrication, but also not clearly consistent with unmediated traumatic recall.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the STRONG band (7.1 WCS). Internal consistency is high with natural memory artifacts. Sensory specificity is rich across multiple channels with strong peripheral detail. Spontaneity and authenticity are exceptionally high—witness demonstrates genuine uncertainty and self-correction throughout. Emotional authenticity is the weakest dimension, scoring 6—emotional content is present but muted, partially explained by generational trauma response patterns and explicit security clearance suppression. Context plausibility is excellent with verifiable details. Corroboration is limited (same-incident witnesses only, with ambiguous uncle testimony). The account exhibits structural hallmarks of genuine experiential memory: sensory richness without over-specification, natural uncertainty about specific details (tail, exact duration), spontaneous self-correction, and credible motivation for prolonged silence. The 30-year disclosure delay with documented security clearance risk is particularly significant—this demonstrates powerful external suppression mechanism consistent with genuine trauma. Military background and disciplined narrative structure enhance rather than diminish credibility in this forensic context, as they explain clear recall patterns. The knuckle-cracking sound and marching gait are distinctive sensory details that reduce likelihood of pure cultural contamination. Primary weakness is emotional under-response relative to encounter intensity, but this aligns with documented Gen X cohort trauma response and military conditioning rather than fabrication patterns.

accounts.scoring_notes: This testimony presents a moderate-credibility profile. Strengths: geographic/contextual coherence, acknowledgment of uncertainty, spontaneity in elaboration, multi-sensory reporting (though visual-dominant), military/professional background providing plausible woodcraft competency, 7-year commitment to narrative. Weaknesses: emotional authenticity poorly demonstrated (intellectual rather than visceral affect), corroboration entirely secondhand/unverified, some sensory claims lack grounding (dream communication), no measurable post-encounter disruption despite claimed terror episodes, consistent geographic/folklore knowledge could support either genuine experience or researched fabrication. The account reads as a careful, epistemically humble narrative with strong behavioral observation consistency rather than a trauma-driven disclosure. Score reflects genuine-seeming memory structure without high confidence in experiential authenticity.

accounts.scoring_notes: Overall credibility is MODERATE. This account exhibits genuine traumatic memory markers (persistent recall, sensory specificity, emotional hedging, reluctance to disclose, comparative anchoring) but lacks high-impact emotional trauma response or external corroboration that would elevate it to STRONG. The witness is articulate, self-aware of implausibility, and provides coherent context. Sensory detail is rich across multiple channels. Internal consistency is good. However, the absence of physical evidence, non-corroborating witness, and the witness's stated lack of lasting life disruption ('hasn't really affected me') create structural inconsistency with severe trauma. The account reads as genuine experienced memory rather than fabrication, but at moderate rather than high confidence. The side narrative about fairy creatures and spiritual framework may enhance or reduce credibility depending on evaluator bias. No dealbreaker contradictions or obvious confabulation markers present.

She thanks the creatures ('I've said thank you before') but this reads as intellectual philosophy rather than emotional catharsis. The closing comments show no post-impact life disruption—she still hikes regularly. The phrase 'I know so many people have' scary experiences suggests some vicarious emotional engagement but detached. Disclosure delay unknown (encounters may be years old). The protective ritual (crystals, prayer) indicates some underlying anxiety, contradicting her flat affect claim.

IC.rationale: The witness account (Brian Terrell) is primarily second-hand testimony about the property owner's experiences. Within his own direct encounters (the two dogman sightings from his past), descriptions are stable and consistent. However, the property investigation narrative contains some minor inconsistencies: the witness states the brush pile encounter happened at '35-40 yards' initially but then revises to 'about 10 feet' of actual advance. The red creature is alternately described as 'wolf-looking' and 'red canine creature' with some ambiguity about species identification. The timeline of events at the property (first night disturbances → tree knocks → trail cameras → various sightings) is coherent but lacks precise dating. Description drift occurs when discussing the two different creatures on the property—initial identification shifts between 'dogman' and 'Sasquatch' based on limited visual information.

Direct sensory detail from the property events is thin: 'herd of elephants moving through woods,' 'tree knocks,' 'growling,' 'calming feeling of peace.' The witness acknowledges minimal sensory engagement on his own investigation visit ('took me out to the location'). Touch, smell, and kinesthetic detail are almost entirely absent. Peripheral context is present (20 acres, forested, ponds, outbuildings, stone walkway) but lacks rich sensory texture. The account is predominantly visual with some audio detail, but lacks the multi-channel specificity expected of direct traumatic memory.

SA.rationale: The testimony is highly structured and largely reactive to interviewer questions. Brian presents a well-organized narrative that flows linearly through topics (his background → his own past encounters → the property investigation → specific property events). There are minimal self-corrections or spontaneous uncertainty admissions. When uncertainty arises, it is stated flatly rather than organically: 'I don't know' appears multiple times but without genuine exploratory tone. The narrative about the property owner's experiences reads as a synthesized report rather than spontaneous disclosure. One notable moment of authenticity: 'I'm not Captain Woo...I struggle with it' suggests some genuine internal conflict, but this is presented as established philosophy rather than in-the-moment disclosure. The witness does not volunteer unexpected details; instead, he answers structured questions in prepared manner. No 'I know how this sounds' type statements. The account structure is unusually neat for traumatic memory—each section concludes cleanly before moving to the next topic.

EA.rationale: Emotional authenticity is muted throughout. Brian reports his own past encounters with clinical detachment: 'I don't think that I've really suffered the level of PTSD that some people have.' He explicitly states discomfort with going into woods alone at night in certain contexts but frames this as practical caution rather than emotional distress. Regarding the property owner's encounters, he relays her reported 'overwhelming feeling of peace' and 'calm' but does not anchor these emotionally—they function more as data points than lived experiences. The bluff charge incident (where son was threatened) is described with narrative interest but without emotional markers of the property owner's fear or Brian's concern for her safety until much later ('I was concerned about them taking it seriously'). There is no evidence of voice quality shifts, emotional breaks at narrative junctures, or physiological stress responses in his telling. The closing statement about wanting 'better, more solid answers' is the only moment approaching genuine emotion, but it is brief and professional. The witness's stated belief in divine protection ('I believe that I have protection from the Lord') may suppress emotional display rather than generate it.

CP.rationale: Context is substantially plausible with minor inconsistencies. Brian's background as a field investigator in Oklahoma/Kansas is coherent and matches his described activities. His past encounters in 'Northwest Oklahoma' during daylight driving scenarios are contextually sound. The property owner's situation is geographically plausible: 20-acre forested property in southeastern Oklahoma with ponds, springs, diverse plantings, and previous owner's horticultural work provides legitimate ecological rationale for animal presence. The 2016 purchase date is temporally consistent with multi-year pattern of activity. However, one contextual oddity: the witness mentions wolves and red wolves historically in Oklahoma, correctly notes their extinction, but then reports current wolf sightings and photographs without explaining the implausibility adequately. His response ('we're not supposed to have wolves here') acknowledges the problem but doesn't resolve it. The property owner's initial attribution to Sasquatch (after 'Finding Bigfoot' media exposure) and gradual identification of a separate aggressive canine creature is contextually plausible. The bluff charge scenario (20-30 yards from house, stone walkway, brush pile) has logical spatial coherence.

He states 'I would say she was probably...she kind of reenacted it for us in the video' but this is reenactment, not original documentation. The property owner reportedly has trail camera footage (wolf at pond, mushroom depletion) but these are not independently verified by Brian or presented as physical evidence. The property owner's son is present during the bluff charge but his account is not independently reported—only filtered through his mother's version relayed to Brian. Neighbors have allegedly discussed wolves ('among themselves' and 'semi-quasi common knowledge') but this is hearsay. The property owner's mother is mentioned as 'armed' and aware, suggesting she may corroborate the property disturbances, but no independent statement from her is presented. accounts.scoring_notes: This is a strong-credibility account structurally. The witness demonstrates authentic trauma markers (delayed fear, shock state, goosebumps upon recollection), genuine sensory specificity with acknowledged gaps, non-defensive uncertainty admissions, and post-encounter behavioral changes consistent with actual experiential memory. The 3-day processing delay before fear/realization is particularly diagnostic of genuine trauma rather than fabrication. Dog's corroborating behavior and witness's appropriate epistemic humility (not claiming to see what he couldn't) strengthen credibility. Main limitations are: no second witness to creature itself, no physical evidence documented as recovered, and solo account status. The account reads as neither polished nor rehearsed—it's meandering, self-correcting, and tangential in ways consistent with genuine memory retrieval under interview pressure. Emotional authenticity is the strongest dimension (9/10) and this carries significant weight in genuine trauma assessment.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the MODERATE range, displaying characteristics consistent with genuine traumatic memory but with significant limitations. Key strengths: (1) high context plausibility, (2) multiple self-corrections and uncertainty admissions suggesting honest memory reconstruction rather than fabrication, (3) detailed kinesthetic/movement descriptions (a hallmark of genuine encounter testimony), (4) post-encounter life disruption (obsession, dog behavioral changes), (5) explicit emotional acknowledgment with situational anchoring. Key weaknesses: (1) primarily uncorroborated solo account, (2) relatively muted emotional expression during interview itself despite claimed trauma, (3) description instability on specific features (ears, hands, tail, final posture) suggesting either poor encoding or genuine memory decay, (4) interviewer-leading effect (witness may be incorporating 'dogman' framework from show exposure), (5) unclear disclosure timeline. The account reads more as genuine-but-limited memory than fabrication, with the struggle to articulate unusual movement patterns being particularly credible. The post-encounter obsessive research behavior is notable credibility marker—fabricators typically show less sustained engagement. However, complete lack of corroboration and the interviewer's suggestive framing prevent a higher score.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the Strong range (7.6 WCS). Emotional authenticity is the clear strength (9/10), with documented physiological stress, post-encounter life disruption, and narrative-anchored emotional breaks. Sensory specificity is exceptional (9/10), with rich multi-channel detail across visual, auditory, olfactory, kinesthetic, and contextual domains. Internal consistency is good (7/10) with minor timeline fluidity but core narrative stability. Spontaneity is strong (8/10) with self-corrections and uncertainty admissions typical of genuine witnesses. Context plausibility is high (8/10) with internally coherent geography and legitimate situational reason for presence. Corroboration is modest (5/10): single co-witness (Seth) acknowledged but not independently interviewed; photographic/video evidence claimed but not verified. The witness's self-identification as a medium introduces potential for confabulation or narrative blending with prior paranormal belief system, but this is not disqualifying for trauma memory assessment. The account does NOT exhibit classic fabrication markers (scripted linearity, uniform affect, resistance to uncertainty). Triggering response to mundane stimuli and sleep disruption are physiologically plausible post-trauma responses. Overall structural alignment with genuine experiential memory is strong, though independent corroboration of the entity itself remains absent.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account sits solidly in Moderate band (6.6/10). Strengths: rich sensory detail across 4+ channels, authentic emotional responses with clear narrative anchoring, substantial post-encounter life disruption (night terrors, drinking, depression, relationship strain), genuine disclosure delay, self-corrections, uncertainty admissions, and plausible situational context. The decision to expose girlfriend to trauma (and her subsequent institutionalization) paradoxically increases credibility—fabricators rarely construct narratives with such high personal/relational costs. Witness demonstrates metacognitive awareness of how account reads to skeptics. Weaknesses: limited corroboration (two named witnesses present, but no independent statements), girlfriend's hospitalization actually undermines her testimony institutionally, witness admits lying to mental health professionals (which could indicate pattern of fabrication or pattern of learned distrust), sensory detail somewhat asymmetrical (visual/tactile/auditory rich, but olfactory/taste absent). The blue flame phenomenon is presented without explanation or follow-up investigation, which is slightly suspicious. Overall: Account reads as genuinely experienced memory rather than fabricated narrative, but has corroboration gaps and reliability concerns stemming from witness's own truth-telling history.

The 5th encounter narrative shows: (1) accurate physiological stress responses anchored to threat exposure, (2) rich multisensory detail particularly in olfactory and kinesthetic channels, (3) appropriate timeline distortion under extreme stress, (4) coherent post-encounter behavioral changes, (5) significant disclosure delay with initial resistance, (6) plausible and internally consistent context. The witness also states 'I didn't know how tall the trees were' yet later provides detailed height comparisons and measurements, suggesting post-hoc rationalization. accounts.scoring_notes: Kirk Reid's testimony scores in the 'Moderate' range (WCS 6.625). Strengths: genuine traumatic memory markers (sensory specificity across 4+ channels, tactile-olfactory detail, physiological freeze response, self-corrections on recall, peripheral environmental consistency, post-encounter life changes, context plausibility). Weaknesses: limited independent corroboration (one secondary witness to one encounter, primary encounter solo), emotional authenticity somewhat muted by intellectual/analytical framing, disclosure not delayed, significant portion of testimony devoted to investigative work on others' cases rather than personal experience narrative, no physical evidence. The account structurally resembles genuine experiential trauma memory in sensory encoding and contextual plausibility, but lacks the emotional intensity and independent corroboration typical of highest-credibility trauma testimony. Score reflects honest, coherent, sensorily-detailed account with moderate to good consistency, but limited external validation and emotional intensity that would push it to 'Strong' category.

IC.rationale: Multiple significant inconsistencies undermine narrative coherence. Michael claims he was in a 'trance' and cannot remember the encounter ('I can't remember anything at this point'), yet provides detailed descriptions of events he supposedly didn't witness. Timeline is problematic: he says he was 'out of it for at least it felt like hours' but 'according to Simon and Ambrie it were about minutes'—yet he describes Anne-Marie being 'chased by the werewolf' and 'stopped by it twice,' details he claims to have learned secondhand. Most critically, he states he picked up 'that peg that were in the fire' and 'started swinging it around' while simultaneously claiming complete dissociation. The narrative shifts between 'I was frozen like a statue' and 'I grabbed the peg and swung it,' which are mutually exclusive states. His knowledge of the fire turning blue, the creature's behavior toward others, and specific tactical details all contradict his stated unconsciousness.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the Moderate-to-Strong range for structural consistency with genuine traumatic memory. Strengths: extensive peripheral sensory detail, spontaneous elaborations, documented behavioral impact, long disclosure delay, physiological stress markers with clear narrative triggers, self-corrections, uncertainty admissions, and coherent contextual framework. Weaknesses: complete absence of corroboration, reliance on solo witness account, lack of direct auditory/olfactory input (though explained rather than avoided), some retrospective theological interpretation overlaying the core narrative. The account does not exhibit script-like quality; rather, it shows tangential associations and responsive elaboration consistent with genuine memory retrieval. Emotional authenticity is moderate—present but somewhat intellectualized in retrospect rather than raw in delivery (inherent limitation of transcript medium). The 19-year delay before public disclosure, combined with documented impact on camping behavior and nightmares, supports genuine experiential memory rather than confabulation or fabrication. No major contradictions identified; minor elaborations are explained contextually (e.g., why brights weren't on, why windows were up).

accounts.scoring_notes: This is a podcast interview transcript with two separate witnesses (Brittany and Brandon). Scoring reflects both accounts equally weighted. Brittany's encounter (early Nov 2019, roadside, brief 30-second observation) demonstrates higher sensory specificity and emotional grounding. Brandon's encounter (circa 2008, extended multi-minute roadside encounter with behavioral observation) demonstrates stronger spontaneity and self-correction. Neither account demonstrates hallmark fabrication signals (scripted perfection, uniform emotional performance, immediate public disclosure, contradictions). Both show appropriate memory uncertainty, post-encounter behavioral impact, and delayed disclosure. IC score reflects 1 minor reconciliation (Brandon's dog-vs-dogman identity) rather than contradiction. SS score is conservative: primarily visual, some peripheral context, no rich multi-sensory encoding. SA score: both spontaneous but interviewer-guided in latter portions. EA score reflects emotional authenticity through behavioral impact and disclosure delay rather than real-time affective display. CP is high (both contextually plausible). CO is limited (one corroborating report of similar 'kangaroo' descriptor; family members told but not independently verified as credible witnesses). WCS (Weighted Credibility Score) = (IC×0.15 + SS×0.15 + SA×0.15 + EA×0.25 + CP×0.15 + CO×0.15) = (7×0.15 + 6×0.15 + 7×0.15 + 6×0.25 + 8×0.15 + 5×0.15) = 1.05 + 0.9 + 1.05 + 1.5 + 1.2 + 0.75 = 6.45 ≈ 6.4. Band: Moderate. This indicates accounts with structural features consistent with genuine memory (appropriate uncertainty, disclosure delay, behavioral impact, contextual coherence) but lacking in high sensory richness, emotional intensity markers, or third-party corroboration needed for 'Strong' band.

notable_patterns: Emotional flattening: Compare intense emotion surrounding California cliff incident ('never been so scared in my life', 'more afraid of that') versus measured tone regarding encounter itself ('unexpected rather than frightening'). This inversion is atypical—primary encounter usually carries greater emotional weight than secondary comparison.
Self-illuminated eyes reported: 'glowing like a yellowish tent'

Conscious avoidance of supernatural framing: Multiple explicit denials of paranormal interpretation ('I don't believe in the supernatural stuff', 'I believe in more like flesh and blood', 'not a ghost'). This may indicate preemptive boundary-setting.
Delayed disclosure: 'took me a couple of days to get the courage' before telling mother, suggesting real emotional processing period.

Minimization language present: 'unexpected' rather than 'terrifying', 'freaked out a little bit or more concerned' (hedging), 'that's probably why I went white as a ghost' (speculative attribution of own reaction).
Pragmatic post-encounter response: Immediately drove home, decompressed with family, resumed normal vigilance patterns. No lasting behavioral change reported beyond normal wildlife awareness.

Strong peripheral detail abundance suggests rehearsal or crystallized narrative (30+ specific geographic/contextual details), though this is consistent with genuine traumatic memory encoding of context.

Interviewer-led detail expansion: Most specific animal descriptions emerge from interviewer questions ('shoulders-up posture?', 'hump on back?', 'knuckles or pads?') rather than spontaneous volunteer, suggesting lower initial confidence in micro-details.

SA.rationale: Multiple spontaneous unprompted details and genuine self-corrections throughout. Witness volunteers extensive biographical context (Boy Scout background, law student, paranormal interest, childhood werewolf fear) without direct prompting. Clear self-corrections: 'I'll just one more detail' and extended clarification of torso size midway through ('Sorry if I explained it wrong'). Multiple uncertainty admissions: 'I don't know how long,' 'I can't measure feet or yards,' 'I couldn't exactly tell what it was,' 'I can't exactly answer why.' Disclosure of intoxication unprompted with concern for credibility: 'I hope this doesn't hurt my credibility...I'm not going to lie.' Non-linear narrative structure with digressions into Portuguese cultural context, historical background of village (Moors, Reconquista), relationship dynamics. However, some later sections become more reactive to interviewer questions (height, head shape, ears). Overall pattern shows substantial spontaneity and authentic uncertainty markers.

EA.rationale: Emotional content present but inconsistently anchored to narrative triggers. Witness demonstrates affect at appropriate moments (fear during initial encounter, unease at girlfriend's house, heart sinking at revelation of girlfriend's denial) but emotional expression is muted throughout the interview. No voice quality shifts evident in transcript. No reported physiological stress responses during the encounter itself (no paralysis, tunnel vision, time distortion reported—instead witness remained calm, curious, observant). The affect appears more cognitive than visceral: 'I felt none of that fear' during encounter, managed decision-making ('I got to keep moving'). Post-encounter impact is significant but delayed: two-year period of avoidance, unconscious unease, persistent rumination ('I still think of it to this day'). Long disclosure delay (2012 encounter, appearing on show at unknown date, 'never revealed to anyone'). However, emotional tone in interview is measured and explanatory rather than distressed. Some emotional authenticity markers (concern about credibility, admission of initial dismissal) but overall affect is controlled, not congruent with typical trauma presentation.

CP.rationale: Context is highly plausible and internally consistent. Legitimate reasons for presence: college student at campus concert, took wrong exit from unfamiliar campus (science campus, not his law campus located elsewhere), decided to walk home at ~1am due to noise curfew. Geographic coherence: multiple exits from campus, ring road system around city, houses/villages accessible by continuing on road, small country with connected road network. Seasonal/temporal plausibility: March/April timeframe (fresh air, walking at night plausible), full moon and street lamps provide illumination logic, college party context with alcohol consumption realistic. Girlfriend's house context: isolated village with mountains/forests, end of village location, attic living arrangement, historical background of village plausible for rural Portugal. No contradictions between setting descriptions. One minor ambiguity: exact timing becomes uncertain ('1am or something' → '2 or 3am') but this is realistic for intoxicated witness and consistent with walking time. Overall, the contextual scaffolding is coherent, detailed, and contextually appropriate for rural Portugal.

Girlfriend's presence at second location (attic incident) is confirmed in that she exists and lived in the village, but she disputes her own involvement in the stairway incident ('I was already asleep. No prior area reports mentioned by witness (though he alludes to roads being 'known to be haunted' and village having 'fateful past'). Overall, corroboration is minimal—driver provides partial contextual support but not witness to the actual encounter.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account presents moderate credibility markers consistent with genuine experiential memory. Strengths: contextual plausibility, sensory specificity across multiple channels, spontaneous unprompted details, self-corrections suggesting memory reconstruction rather than rehearsal, long disclosure delay, post-encounter behavioral change (avoidance, unease). Weaknesses: controlled emotional affect atypical of trauma trauma presentation, intoxication (though witness claims sobriety achieved through walking), lack of corroborating witnesses to actual encounter, girlfriend's contradictory account on second incident. The transcript quality is poor (transcription errors, fragmented speech) which somewhat obscures emotional vocal markers. The 12+ year delay between encounters and disclosure, combined with behavioral evidence of impact (avoidance of village, seeking answers), suggest genuine memory processing rather than fabrication. However, the moderate (not strong) scores across dimensions reflect that this is a plausible account rather than a definitively authentic one. Key uncertainty: the extent to which cultural expectations (Portuguese paranormal traditions, interviewer specialization in dogman/cryptid reports) influenced narrative framing.

accounts.scoring_notes: This testimony presents as MODERATE credibility when scored against genuine traumatic memory structures. Strengths: plausible geographic context, specific environmental detail (trees, terrain features, fire hydrants), moderate sensory specificity, and genuine uncertainty admissions. Weaknesses: lack of emotional/physiological acute stress markers, minimal corroboration despite claimed companions, heavy post-hoc philosophical reframing, explicit acknowledgment of expectancy bias ('I put out a certain vibration'), and apparent comfort/detachment during encounters. The witness demonstrates the communication style of an experienced storyteller and educator (clear transitions, engagement with interviewer) rather than someone struggling to articulate fresh trauma. Post-encounter life narrative emphasizes meaning-making and purpose-discovery rather than PTSD, hypervigilance from fear, or relational disruption. The 'healing' framing and reflective tone suggest these narratives serve a current psychological function (purpose, community, identity) rather than represent unprocessed traumatic material. Score reflects moderate structural resemblance to genuine memory with notable gaps in authentic trauma markers.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account presents a paradox: rich sensory detail and coherent spatial context for the encounter moment, but weak emotional authenticity and poor temporal anchoring. The witness demonstrates sophisticated theological reasoning and narrative consistency at the macro level, but internal contradictions on dates and emotional response patterns suggest post-hoc construction or confabulation rather than direct traumatic memory. The 2014 head injury episode—with its vivid visionary detail, emotional breaks, and well-documented aftermath (hospitalization, disability, years of recovery)—is structurally far more consistent with genuine traumatic memory than the dogman encounter itself. The dogman sighting is presented as a brief, emotionally flat, witnessed moment that the witness later researched and contextualized through internet sources. This inversion of emotional intensity (intense on the NDE, flat on the encounter) and the extensive post-encounter intellectual elaboration suggest the dogman narrative may be a meaning-making construction layered onto an ambiguous event (missing cat) rather than retrieval of vivid sensory memory. The absence of typical trauma markers (fear, shock, physiological arousal) is particularly notable for an alleged supernatural predation event.

IC.rationale: Core account is stable and internally coherent. The encounter narrative itself is consistent: animal emerges from cornfield, stands in road, leaps across lanes into woods. However, there is one notable inconsistency: witness describes the encounter as occurring in 'slow motion' and states the animal 'stood in the road for a minute or two,' yet also describes scanning its body during the leap itself—the timeline of these simultaneous actions is slightly unclear. Minor drift in peripheral details (e.g., uncertainty about exact time: 'quarter to four' vs 'by 4:30, quarter to five'). These are natural memory artifacts rather than substantive contradictions.

accounts.scoring_notes: Account scores in Moderate band (6.8/10). Structural authenticity markers present: sensory specificity is strong (8/10), particularly kinesthetic detail of anomalous movement; internal consistency stable with natural uncertainty (7/10); context highly plausible (9/10). Weaknesses in emotional authenticity (6/10) — affect measured, possibly constrained by interview format — and corroboration (5/10) limited to single-witness-plus-secondary testimony without independent creature documentation. Spontaneity moderate (7/10): practiced narrative suggests repeated telling but with genuine hesitations and self-correction. No paralysis or missing time during primary sighting (differentiates from classic abduction narrative). Secondary encounter introduces audio frequency component and physiological cascade (nausea, disorientation, urinary urgency, time loss) with prayer resolution — element that resists straightforward frequency explanation. Overall assessment: account exhibits forensic markers of genuine experiential memory (specific sensory encoding, appropriate uncertainty, self-correction, contextual coherence) rather than fabrication, but limited independent corroboration prevents higher confidence scoring. Witness appears sincere but active research operator, potentially subject to pattern-matching confirmation bias in ongoing field work.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the 'Strong' range (7.6 WCS). The encounter narrative demonstrates structural authenticity markers: rich sensory specificity tied to a single modality (visual) with acknowledged sensory gaps (no smell due to wind direction), appropriate emotional anchoring to narrative moments, spontaneous detail with natural self-correction, and significant post-encounter life reorganization (complete avoidance of nighttime driving, structured daytime-only activities). Internal consistency is high with only minor, naturally-resolved discrepancies about creature's startlement level. Context plausibility is excellent with detailed geographic and temporal coherence. Corroboration is moderate: documented co-witness at location (though not present at encounter moment), independent fisherman report of similar creature, documentary and interview evidence of witness's credibility-building efforts. The account lacks high-impact emotional breaks (no crying, no voice cracking) and physical evidence, which prevents a higher score. The witness's explanation for lack of photography (startle response, survival instinct) is psychologically plausible but untested. Overall, the account resembles genuine experiential trauma memory more than fabrication, though the interview format and retrospective processing (10 years post-encounter) introduces some mediation through repeated telling.

One potential inconsistency: witness states he 'never really had a chance to talk to him [father] about that that night' because father was asleep, but then mentions father 'had gotten up in a bad mood' the next day—the implication that father didn't know about the incident that night is clear, but the phrasing could confuse. Overall, core details remain stable.

Kinesthetic: 'every organ, it shook it,' sensation of physical impact. Tactile: description of dirt on the rock, thickness of fingernails. Environmental peripheral detail: hot dogs on skewers, tents in triangle, fire pit, wood pile, foliage movement. Temporal specificity: 'probably 10 o'clock, maybe 10:30.' One minor limitation: smell and taste not substantially engaged, which prevents a 9-10 score.

EA.rationale: Strong emotional authenticity with clear narrative anchoring. Witness demonstrates physiological stress response appropriate to trauma (jump-scare sensitivity: 'I can't have people jump scare me...you might get punched'; nightmares for years; avoidance of woods for 26 years until 'a couple days ago'; behavioral changes observed by parents). Like, this is the beginning of them'; visible emotional intensity when describing the scream and its life-altering effect. Post-encounter life impact clearly articulated: 'distrust, paranoia,' therapy for 5 years, inability to re-enter woods. Long disclosure delay (26 years before public testimony). Voice quality markers present (hesitation, emphasis on key moments). However, in interview format, emotional display is somewhat modulated rather than raw; the most authentic moments are reflective rather than immediate. The discussion of brother's death shows emotional weight but is tangential to the encounter itself.

accounts.scoring_notes: Account demonstrates structural consistency with genuine traumatic memory. Strengths: rich environmental detail in Afghanistan encounter, natural uncertainty admissions, spontaneous elaborations, plausible context across all locations, emotional responses anchored to narrative moments, post-encounter behavior modification (avoiding solo night walks, defensive base routing, extended hygiene ritual). Weaknesses: limited corroboration (one partial witness, one tertiary report), single uncorroborated Afghanistan encounter, some sensory details attributed to friend rather than witness (friend smelled something, witness did not), muted emotional affect regarding potential danger (intellectualized as impractical). The asymmetry between friend's strong reaction (1997) and witness's lack of sensation warrants scrutiny but is not necessarily inconsistent with genuine memory—selective perception during stress is documented. Afghanistan encounter lacks the immediate physiological stress markers (paralysis, time distortion) typical of highest-confidence traumatic encoding, though anxiety response was present. Overall profile suggests authentic experiential memory with realistic memory limitations and natural disclosure hesitancy.

accounts.scoring_notes: This transcript is a live-stream interview, not a spontaneous encounter testimony. Brian Terrell is a dogman researcher being questioned by a host. His own encounter account is fragmented across responses to unrelated questions and constrained by interview format. The core sighting details (size, appearance, shock) are consistent but lack the emotional intensity, sensory richness, and narrative spontaneity expected of genuine traumatic memory. The account reads as a professional recounting of a past event rather than someone reliving or processing trauma. The emotional authenticity score is particularly low—no vocal breaks, tears, or physiological markers occur when discussing his actual encounter, only when discussing research philosophy. The disclosure occurred years ago and is now professionally normalized. No evidence of post-encounter disruption to his life trajectory—he became a researcher, suggesting integration rather than ongoing distress. Corroboration is minimal and secondhand. This profile is consistent with either a genuine but psychologically processed experience presented in professional context, OR a narrative that has been refined and rationalized through years of research and public positioning. Cannot distinguish between these alternatives from this material alone.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account presents as a coherent, plausible narrative with good situational grounding and stable core details. However, it exhibits structural markers more consistent with retrospective narrative construction than acute traumatic memory. The emotional dampening is the most significant indicator: a witness encountering five large unidentified bipedal canines at close range (300 feet) should display physiological stress markers, yet the witness explicitly frames this as preferable to her earlier 50-year-old encounter because it was 'nice' and allowed comfortable observation. The lack of adrenaline response, post-encounter life disruption, or delayed disclosure (repeat guest on podcast) weakens emotional authenticity. Spontaneity is limited; the witness operates largely in reactive/response mode. Sensory detail is adequate but narrow (primarily visual) and lacks the involuntary hyper-specificity of trauma (what was she wearing, exact body sensations, involuntary sounds). Context plausibility is excellent, which may paradoxically support schema-driven narrative (familiar roads, familiar activity, fits prior framework). The account reads as a responsible, observed recounting of an unusual event rather than a struggle to articulate a destabilizing experience. This may indicate genuine observation of unfamiliar animals—which would produce measured rather than traumatized response—or may indicate narrative stability achieved through conscious structuring. The Moderate band reflects genuine observational consistency offset by absent emotional and spontaneity markers characteristic of true traumatic encoding.

IC.rationale: Account maintains general chronological coherence across multiple incidents (1982 coyote incident, 1992 encounters, 1993-94 encounters, 2004 encounters). However, significant timeline inconsistencies exist. Keith states the 1982 incident occurred when he was 'seven, eight years old' placing it at '82, 83 roughly,' but then discusses 1992 as 'summer vacation of my junior year in high school' and 1993-94 as 'senior year,' which would place his birth around 1974-1975, making him 7-8 in 1981-1982, not 1982-1983. Furthermore, he frequently loses temporal specificity ('I'm gonna mess up this time here,' 'I don't remember if it was winter,' 'I think it was 92'). Description consistency is stronger—physical details of the creatures remain stable across encounters (tall, canine head, long snout, glowing white or red eyes, bowed front legs, short gray hair). The account includes self-corrections typical of genuine memory ('I mean, I got a hound dog, a little beagle') but also contains vague patches where he acknowledges memory failure.

accounts.scoring_notes: Account scores in MODERATE band primarily due to strong sensory specificity and plausible context offsetting consistency issues and limited corroboration. High sensory detail (8/10) is credibility strength. Emotional authenticity is genuine but not dramatically realized (7/10). Spontaneity is present but somewhat formulaic given interview format (7/10). Internal consistency issues stem from temporal confusion rather than narrative contradictions—core account stable (6/10). Context highly plausible (8/10). Corroboration limited to single witnesses per event with one indirect confirmation (4/10). The account manifests genuine memory markers (sensory richness, emotional anchoring, self-correction) alongside limitations of a 40-year retrospective narrative. Witness demonstrates sophisticated meta-awareness of credibility concerns ('I know how this sounds' equivalent) without defensive deflection. No evidence of fabrication, but also no falsifiability—accounts are inherently unfalsifiable solo observations. Weighted emotional authenticity score (7/10 × 1.25 = 8.75 adjusted) elevates overall credibility assessment due to physiologically-grounded stress responses and clear trigger-emotion pairing.

He describes a creature with a mane as 'kind of cool looking too,' which contradicts the threat model. The account reads as intellectually organized testimony rather than fragmented traumatic memory. Notably, the host's meta-commentary ('I didn't hear one that sounded like it was traumatic') is the strongest piece of external observation available, and it cuts against authenticity. While internal consistency is high (scoring IC at 6), this may reflect scripting rather than genuine memory consolidation. The Emotional Authenticity score of 3 is weighted at 25% and drives the overall WCS band to 'Weak' (3.96). This is consistent with a rehearsed, non-traumatic account presented in a paranormal entertainment context.

accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the Strong range (7.3 WCS). Primary strengths: robust sensory specificity across multiple channels (8/10), strong emotional authenticity anchored to narrative moments with physiological markers (8/10), and excellent context plausibility (9/10). Internal consistency is good (7/10) with one date correction but stable core narrative. Spontaneity is moderate-to-good (7/10) with natural uncertainty admissions but somewhat reactive structure. Corroboration is limited to immediate family dismissal (3/10), which paradoxically may increase authenticity as genuine witnesses often face disbelief. Long disclosure delay (28 years) and documented behavioral changes in both witness and companion animal are consistent with genuine traumatic memory. The account lacks smell reporting and contains some movie-comparison scaffolding post-hoc, which could indicate confabulation influence, but visual and kinesthetic detail is primary and experientially coherent. The protective entity hypothesis emerging naturally during interview (good vs. bad dogman) shows cognitive complexity. Overall structure resembles genuine experiential memory with appropriate trauma markers.

accounts.scoring_notes: This is a Q&A panel discussion, not a direct witness testimony, which complicates assessment. The format is performative (live stream, multiple guests, host moderation), introducing rehearsal effects. However, both Mike and Paul demonstrate structural markers of genuine traumatic memory: (1) emotional authenticity anchored to specific narrative moments (Mike's post-encounter fear behaviors, Paul's hunting disruption); (2) appropriate disclosure delay (years between encounters and public discussion); (3) spontaneous self-correction and uncertainty admission; (4) tangential theorizing unprompted by questions; (5) sensory specificity across visual and kinesthetic channels, though limited olfactory/gustatory detail. Weaknesses: (1) temporal vagueness (consistent with trauma but exploitable); (2) no independently verified corroboration (private groups, refused public evidence); (3) narrative reinforcement between speakers creates mutual validation loop; (4) some elements suggest post-hoc theorizing (portal discussion, dimensional theories) layered onto core encounter; (5) context plausibility has minor gaps. The account's strength lies in emotional-physiological consistency (fear behaviors, processing recovery, life disruption) rather than narrative clarity. This pattern—high emotional authenticity, lower corroboration—is consistent with either genuine trauma or sophisticated role-play by individuals familiar with trauma narratives. The refusal to release evidence, framed as social protection, is authentic to genuine experiencers but also limits falsifiability. Recommend conservative 'Moderate' band: sufficient internal coherence and emotional texture to suggest genuine experience, but insufficient corroboration and some narrative drift prevent higher confidence.

notable_patterns: Reliance on video slow-motion verification rather than direct sensory memory ('slow them down to literally a frame a second and you'll see them')
Habituated-witness framework: repeated visits normalize threat, eliminate startle responses—atypical of true encounter trauma
Extensive theoretical elaboration (species hierarchy, demarcation lines, seasonal patterns, bioengineering hypothesis) suggests post-encounter cognitive integration rather than raw memory
Emotional flatness despite high-stakes narrative (invisible circling predator, fatal risk, potential ambush)
Multiple vague temporal markers ('I can't remember which date precisely,' 'this one time,' 'last November') inconsistent with vivid traumatic encoding
Feeding behavior (pork chops, sausages, treats) and 'relationship-building' language ('give-and-take relationship,' 'peace offering') frames encounters as investigative rather than traumatic
Heavy use of passive-voice discovery ('I found a cub in their den,' 'I've been there,' video analysis) vs. active sensory immersion

it doesn't phase me
accounts.scoring_notes: This account presents as a seasoned researcher's integrated narrative rather than traumatic memory. The absence of emotional break, minimal sensory specificity beyond the visual channel, reliance on video frame-by-frame verification, and theoretical elaboration all suggest Mike is reconstructing a coherent investigative history rather than reporting raw experiential memories. The 'habituation' framework itself is unusual: genuine trauma victims typically do not become comfortable with their aggressors through repeated exposure in ways they explicitly recount as normalized. The steady timeline of visits (early hours, weekends, 'family concerns'), the strategic feeding pattern, the demarcation-line geography, and the multi-location comparative analysis suggest this is a well-integrated quasi-professional fieldwork narrative. Moderate score reflects that the account is internally plausible and not contradictory, but structurally inconsistent with how genuine traumatic memory encodes in human cognition. The video evidence and claimed corroborators (Danny, Dean Kooling, sister location) add surface plausibility but remain unverified. Emotional authenticity is the weakest dimension—no genuine affect markers at moments of high threat.

IC.rationale: Account is largely internally consistent in sequence and major details. However, there is a significant logical inconsistency regarding sensory evidence: Cody hears scratching on the wall and footsteps on the roof, but finds no physical evidence the next morning. He attempts to rationalize this ('maybe he was just running his hands across it'), but this explanation sits uneasily with the intensity of the described experience. Additionally, the claim that his partner and dogs slept through roof thumping while he remained awake is presented as fact without acknowledgment of how unusual this is, though he does express some self-doubt about whether events actually occurred. Minor timeline gaps exist (exact duration of roof activity unclear). The core narrative structure holds, but the disconnect between vivid sensory experience and complete absence of physical evidence creates mild strain.

Kinesthetic/proprioceptive: details about own body state ('my heart stops', 'I freeze', 'I'm just shaking', 'panicking in my head'). Peripheral context strong: small dogs' behavior, specific porch lighting setup, backyard layout, fence heights, neighbor configuration. Strong environmental anchoring.

accounts.scoring_notes: This transcript is fundamentally incomplete for forensic credibility assessment. The critical narrative—the actual encounter—is deferred ('which we'll get to in a bit'). What is present (context, emotional response to encounter, physiological markers, disclosure pathway) scores HIGH on authenticity markers. What is absent (the encounter itself) cannot be scored. The account reads as genuine trauma response to *something*, but the something is not described here. The witness demonstrates hallmarks of genuine distress: family corroboration, physiological evidence, meaningful disclosure hesitation, post-encounter life concern, emotional breaks appropriate to narrative moment. However, the structure of the interview (radio show format, host affirmation, deferred encounter detail) creates conditions that could support either genuine memory or polished retelling. The sensory specificity score is artificially lowered because the encounter narrative is literally not present. If the encounter detail were included and matched the quality of pre/post-encounter detail, SS would likely score 7-8. Overall WCS places account in MODERATE range: structurally consistent with genuine traumatic memory in terms of emotional and contextual markers, but incomplete sensory corroboration and interview format constraints prevent higher confidence.
